privacy.title: Privacy Policy
paragraphs: Effective Date: December 29, 2025Last Updated: December 29, 2025

Welcome to Pocket Fit (“we,” “us,” or “our”). We are committed to protecting your privacy. This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, disclose, and safeguard your information when you visit our website [https://www.pocket-fit.app] and use our services, including our mobile application (collectively, the “Service”).
1. Information We Collect
We may collect and process the following types of personal data:
a. Personal Information- Name- Email address- Account login credentials (if applicable)
b. Fitness & Health Data- Workout logs and performance- User-input fitness goals- Biometric data you optionally provide
c. Technical Data- IP address- Browser type and device information- Operating system- Usage patterns and interaction logs
2. How We Use Your Information

We use your personal data to:- Provide and maintain the Service- Personalize your workout experience- Communicate updates, offers, and service notices- Conduct analytics to improve the Service- Ensure security and prevent misuse- Comply with legal obligations
3. Sharing Your Information

We may share your information with:- Service providers (e.g., hosting, analytics, and customer support tools)- Integrated partners (e.g., calorie-tracking apps you connect to Pocket Squats)- Authorities, when legally required- Business partners in the event of a merger, acquisition, or sale of assets
We do not sell your personal data.
4. Legal Basis for Processing (GDPR)

If you are located in the UK or EU, we only process your personal data when:- You have given us consent- It is necessary to perform our contract with you- It is required for compliance with a legal obligation- It is in our legitimate interests and does not override your rights
5. Data Retention
We keep your data for as long as your account is active or as needed to provide you with our services. You can request deletion of your personal data at any time by contacting us.
6. Your Rights

Depending on your jurisdiction, you may have the right to:- Access a copy of the personal data we hold about you- Correct inaccuracies in your data- Request deletion of your personal information- Object to or restrict processing- Withdraw consent at any time (where processing is based on consent)- Request data portability
To exercise any of these rights, please email general@pocketsquats.com.
7. Security
We implement industry-standard security measures to protect your personal information. However, no system is 100% secure, and we cannot guarantee absolute protection.
8. Children’s Privacy

Pocket Squats is not intended for users under the age of 16. We do not knowingly collect or solicit data from children. If we learn that we have collected personal data from a child under 16, we will delete it promptly.
9. International Transfers
Your data may be stored and processed in countries outside your own, including the UK, EU, or United States. We take appropriate measures to protect your data in line with applicable privacy laws.
10. Changes to This Privacy Policy
We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. If changes are significant, we will notify users by email or a notice on our website. Please check this page regularly for updates.
Last Updated:  December 29, 2025
11. Contact Us
If you have any questions or concerns about this Privacy Policy or your data, please contact us:
Email: general@pocket-fit.app

blog.title: Workout accountability without shame: your streak is counting the wrong thing
blog.description: A daily streak quietly punishes the person who trains three or four times a week, which is almost everyone. Change the unit to the week and a missed Tuesday stops being a failure.
blog.body:
You had a 41 day streak. Then work ran late on a Wednesday, and the next morning the number said 1.

Nothing about your training changed. You trained four times that week, the same as the week before and the week before that. But the app decided that four sessions across seven days was a broken chain, and the number that had been quietly congratulating you for six weeks went back to the start. Most people, at that point, do not train harder. They open the app less, then they stop, then they delete it.

The reasonable assumption is that this is a motivation problem, and that the fix is more encouragement: another push notification, a badge, a nudge at 7pm. That is backwards. The streak did not fail to motivate you. It measured you against a target you never set, found you wanting, and told you so in a font size big enough to hurt. The honest fix is not more notifications. It is changing the unit of measurement and the words wrapped around it.

**[Train with an app that counts weeks, not guilt](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** Pocket Fit's streak counts weeks in which you hit your own weekly workout target, and it banks grace weeks so one bad week does not erase a good year. Free on [the App Store](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats), no card needed.

## The daily streak is a measurement error dressed as motivation

Start with arithmetic rather than psychology.

Almost nobody who lifts trains seven days a week. The common patterns are three, four or five sessions, with the rest of the week given to recovery, work and a life. An upper/lower runner trains four. Those are not lapses. They are the plan, and usually the correct plan.

Now apply a daily streak to that person. Every rest day is a zero. The counter either resets constantly, which makes it meaningless, or it quietly redefines the goal as "do something every day", which is a different and worse training programme. A metric that goes red when you follow your own programme correctly is not measuring adherence. It is measuring attendance, and attendance is not what makes you stronger.

This is why Pocket Fit's streak is counted in weeks. A week counts when you hit the number of workouts you told the app you wanted, your own target, not ours. Train four times when you said four and the week is green. Train four when you said three and it is still green. Miss the target and the week does not count, but the day you did not train is not an event at all. Against a weekly target, the three to four times a week lifter is finally scored on the thing they are actually trying to do.

## What the habit research actually found about the missed day

The folk wisdom is that a chain, once broken, is broken. The most cited real-world habit study says something far less dramatic.

*Lally, van Jaarsveld, Potts and Wardle* followed **96 volunteers** for 12 weeks for the *European Journal of Social Psychology*, with 82 providing enough data to model. Each person picked one eating, drinking or activity behaviour and did it daily in a fixed context, rating automaticity every day. Two numbers get quoted from this paper. The first is the famous one: the median time to reach 95% of an individual's automaticity plateau was **66 days**, with a range of **18 to 254 days**, which is a polite way of saying that "21 days to build a habit" is fiction.

The second number is the one nobody quotes, and it is the important one. When the authors compared the rise in automaticity across two days where the behaviour was performed on both, against two days with a missed opportunity in between, **missing one opportunity did not materially affect habit formation**. The curve did not reset. It barely flinched.

So the daily streak is not modelling reality. Reality is a slowly rising curve that survives a missed day without noticing. The streak is a step function that treats one absence as collapse. If you have ever felt that a broken streak did more damage than the missed session, that is because it did.

## Gamification works, and then it quietly stops working

We are not against game mechanics. We are against pretending they are free.

*Mazeas, Duclos, Pereira and Chalabaev* pooled **16 randomised controlled trials with 2,407 participants** for the *Journal of Medical Internet Research*. Gamified interventions beat controls on physical activity with a summary effect of **g = 0.42 (95% CI 0.14 to 0.69)**, small to medium and genuinely worth having. Against inactive controls the effect was g = 0.58. Against active, non-gamified interventions it dropped to **g = 0.23**, so a decent chunk of the benefit is simply "an intervention exists" rather than "points exist". At follow-up, averaging about 14 weeks later, the effect fell to **g = 0.15**. Still positive, but the authors are candid that this is consistent with a novelty effect.

Read that as a product decision. Points and badges buy you a few months of engagement. If the mechanic also carries a punishment, you are borrowing motivation early and repaying it later, at the exact moment the novelty wears off and the user needs a reason to keep going. A daily streak is the most expensive version of that loan, because its punishment lands on the ordinary, well-programmed week.

## What we built instead: weeks, shields and a status that can say paused

Here is the actual mechanism, because "we made it kinder" is not an answer.

**The unit is a goal-met week.** Your streak counts consecutive weeks in which your qualified sessions reached your own weekly target. Nothing about days.

**Grace weeks are earned, not given.** For every two consecutive goal-met weeks you bank one grace week, up to three held at once. They are not bought and not handed out on install. You earn them by being consistent, and they exist to be spent on the week when you cannot be.

**They are spent automatically.** When an off week appears in your history, a banked grace week bridges it and the chain continues. You do not have to notice, tap anything, or apologise to a modal. The whole thing is derived on read from your session history, so there is no separate ledger waiting to disagree with reality.

**There are three statuses.** *Active* is a healthy streak. *Shielded* means a grace week is currently holding your chain up, and hitting your goal this week banks it properly. *Paused* means the chain lapsed beyond what your grace could cover.

Note what the third status is not called. It is not lost, broken or failed.

**[Bank a grace week with Pocket Fit](https://www.pocket-fit.app/pricing).** Two consecutive weeks at your own target earns one, up to three, and they are spent for you when a bad week arrives. Free on iOS and Android.

## Paused, not lost: the bet we made on wording

This next part is a design opinion, and we would rather say so plainly than dress it up as clinical evidence.

When a streak really does lapse beyond grace, the app reframes it as paused. The reasoning we wrote into the code comes from Lisa Feldman Barrett's *How Emotions Are Made*: the self is not a fixed object being reported on, it is a concept your brain constructs from prediction and context. If that is even roughly right, a big red zero is not neutral information about your calendar. It is a statement about the kind of person you are, delivered by something you invited into your pocket. The cascade that follows is not "I should train tomorrow". It is "this is not for me", and the next action is uninstall.

We cannot prove that "paused" retains more users than "lost". Nobody has run that trial on our app, and this is a framing choice made on principle, not a measured result. What we can say is that the all or nothing alternative has a well-documented shape in the self-regulation literature. It is the "what the hell" effect described by *Cochran and Tesser*: once a goal is visibly violated, the violation itself licenses further abandonment. Dieters who break the diet eat more, not less. A daily streak manufactures exactly that goal violation every rest day.

One piece of adjacent evidence is worth weight. *Sirois, Kitner and Hirsch* meta-analysed **15 independent samples, N = 3,252**, for *Health Psychology* and found self-compassion positively associated with health-promoting behaviours including exercise, average **r = .25**, with low variability across samples and mediation running partly through affect. It is correlational and self-reported, so it cannot tell you that being kinder to yourself makes you train more. It does make the opposite design choice look strange. Building a core loop that generates self-criticism, in a domain where self-criticism is at best neutral, is a decision that needs defending, and we could not defend it.

## The achievements we deleted

The most concrete way to prove you believe this is to break your own toys.

Pocket Fit used to award day-streak badges: 3-Day, 7-Day, 14-Day and 30-Day Streak. They are no longer awarded at all. Not softened, not rebalanced. The constants still exist in the codebase for exactly one reason, so a badge somebody genuinely earned two years ago still renders with its name instead of a blank card. New ones are never issued.

What replaced them is a Consistency ladder measured in goal-met weeks: **First Week Solid, 2 Weeks Straight, 1 Month Straight, 2 Months Straight, 6 Months Straight, 1 Year Strong**. Every rung is achievable by someone who trains three times a week and rests four, which was never true of the thing it replaced.

The other two categories measure things that only ever go up. **Grind** counts total qualified sessions: 1, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, and a cumulative total cannot be reset by a bad fortnight because the sessions happened. **Strength** counts distinct exercises where you have set a personal best, First Personal Best and then 10 Personal Bests, which rewards range and nudges you towards logging properly rather than towards showing up daily.

## Notifications: four categories, one master switch, and a rule about tone

The second half of the shame loop is the notification. Most fitness apps take a blanket permission at install and then spend it however they like, which is why so many people turn everything off after a fortnight and never see the one message they would have wanted.

Pocket Fit has exactly four user-facing categories, each with its own toggle, plus a master switch that overrides all of them.

**1. Workout reminders.** A reminder for a scheduled workout that names the workout, a nudge when nothing is scheduled asking whether you are training today, and a note for the first workout of the week.

**2. Inactivity nudges**, at day 3, 7, 14 and 30. This is where the recategorisation principle does the most work. The copy does not tell you what you lost. Rest is framed as recovery your body banked, because at day 3 that is usually literally true. The copy also carries tone variants by audience, with a gender-neutral fallback whenever gender is unknown, because a line that lands as friendly ribbing for one person reads as a lecture to another.

**3. Progress and achievements.** Weekly goal hit; 2 and 4 consecutive goal-met weeks; a personal best, naming the exercise; session milestones at 10, 25, 50 and 100; a monthly recap with your workouts, your PRs, the month and a mood lift percentage; body projection check-ins at 4 weeks, 2 months and 3 months; achievement unlocks; and avatar stage unlocks, including a nudge when you are one step from evolving and a progress photo would get you there.

**4. Social.** Friend requests, a friend starting a workout, a friend completing one, and the four peer reactions below.

Two things about the plumbing matter more than they sound. Everything runs as timezone-aware background jobs, so the workout reminder, the inactivity nudge, the weekly goal and consecutive weeks messages, the monthly recap and the body projection check-in all land in your local morning rather than the server's. And the categories exist so you can keep the one you want and kill the rest. Friends' activity and nothing else is two taps. The personal best celebration and none of the nudging is two taps the other way. All or nothing permission is how apps end up with a notification budget of zero.

**[Keep the nudge you want, kill the rest](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).** Four categories, four toggles, one master switch, all delivered in your own timezone. Free on iOS and Android.

## Peer accountability lands differently from app guilt

*Rackow, Scholz and Hornung* ran an 8-week randomised online study for the *British Journal of Health Psychology* in which the intervention was, essentially, get an exercise companion and train with them. Received **emotional** support in the intervention group predicted self-efficacy, self-monitoring and action planning, and was indirectly connected to exercise through those mediators. Received **instrumental** support, the practical kind, showed no significant effect. In the control group, neither type was associated with anything.

Two lessons fall straight out of that. Support has to be received, not merely available, which means a static friends list does nothing. And the useful kind is emotional rather than logistical: somebody caring that you trained beats somebody handing you a spreadsheet. That is why Pocket Fit's social layer is built around live events and light-touch reactions rather than a profile page.

**Friends and follows** are separate models, so you can follow someone whose training you like without a mutual relationship, and keep a friends list for people whose opinion you care about.

**Four peer reactions**, which are the genuinely fun part:

- **Well Done**: your friend is cheering you on, great workout today.
- **Encourage**: your friend is cheering you on, finish strong. Sent mid-session, while it can still change what you do.
- **Poke**: your friend is thinking of you, it has been N days.
- **Laugh**: your friend says your gym shoes are gathering dust, go prove them wrong.

Read that last one again and notice how differently it lands from a system message telling you that you failed. It is the same information. The difference is that a friend has standing to rib you and an algorithm does not. Light ribbing from someone who knows you is a joke you can answer by training. The identical sentiment from an app is a verdict. Friends starting and completing workouts is also what makes the social side live rather than a static profile page, and every one of these pushes sits in that single togglable category.

Around the reactions sits the rest of it. A **post feed** with images, likes and comments, so a session becomes something you share rather than a number you hide. **Groups** you can create, browse, join through join requests that are accepted or declined, or enter by invite, with members, roles, group posts, leaving and removal. And **leaderboards** with a metric, a scope and a time window, where the scope is either global or one specific group, so you can compete inside a group of eight friends rather than against the entire internet. A leaderboard you can plausibly win is a game. A leaderboard with two million people on it is a rejection letter.

Privacy is the boundary that makes it safe: session history visibility is a user setting, so sharing is opt-in rather than assumed.

## Where this evidence is thin

We would rather point at the holes ourselves.

- **Nobody has trialled our streak.** There is no randomised comparison of week-based versus day-based streaks in a real fitness app, ours included. The unit change is an argument from arithmetic and from adjacent literature, not a measured retention result.
- **The Barrett framing is a design principle, not a clinical claim.** *How Emotions Are Made* is a theory of how emotions are constructed. Applying it to push notification copy is our extrapolation, and it deserves to be labelled as one.
- **The self-compassion evidence is correlational.** Sirois and colleagues pooled cross-sectional samples with self-reported behaviour, average r = .25. Causation could run either way: people training consistently may simply find it easier to be kind to themselves.
- **The habit study is small and behaviour-general.** 96 volunteers, 82 analysed, and the behaviours were mostly things like eating fruit at lunch, not squatting. The 18 to 254 day range around a 66 day median shows how much individual variation there is.
- **The gamification meta-analysis is honest about its own limits.** Sixteen trials is not many, the interval on g = 0.42 runs from 0.14 to 0.69, and the drop to g = 0.15 could be novelty, intervention withdrawal, or both.
- **The support study does not report a headline effect size** we can quote you, and its outcome was self-reported exercise over eight weeks. It is suggestive about the mechanism, not decisive about the size.
- **We are not neutral.** We sell the app in this article. Read the numbers, not our enthusiasm.

## What to actually do this week

Four moves, in the order we would do them.

- **Set a weekly target you would hit in a bad week, not a good one.** Three is a real number that produces real progress. Your streak is scored against it, so setting it aspirationally high is how you manufacture your own failure.
- **Write the sessions into specific slots.** *Gollwitzer and Sheeran* pooled **94 independent tests** in *Advances in Experimental Social Psychology* and found if-then implementation intentions, plans naming a when and a where, improved goal attainment with **d = .65**, a medium to large effect. "Train three times" is a wish. "Tuesday 7am, Thursday 7am, Saturday 10am" is a plan. Pocket Fit schedules one day slot per workout so the plan lives somewhere other than your intentions.
- **Log the sets.** *Michie, Abraham, Whittington, McAteer and Gupta* meta-regressed **122 evaluations, N = 44,747**, for *Health Psychology*. The pooled effect on physical activity and healthy eating was **0.31**, and self-monitoring explained more heterogeneity than any other single technique. Interventions combining self-monitoring with at least one other control-theory technique hit **0.42** against **0.26** for everything else. Logging is not admin. It is the intervention.
- **Add two friends, not two hundred.** The reactions only work if the sender knows you. Then set the leaderboard scope to that group.

## Put it together

Two things decide whether you are still training in six months, and neither is the number on a badge. The first is whether your programme fits the life you actually have. We wrote the [full guide to every training split and how Pocket Fit builds a programme](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/how-pocket-fit-builds-your-program-every-training-split-explained) for exactly that, and if you already have a plan you like, you can [bring your own programme in and let the app fill the blanks](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/import-your-own-workout-program) instead of starting again. Progression works the same way throughout: [reps climb first, then the weight moves](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/progressive-overload-reps-first-then-weight), only after a plateau is confirmed.

The second is whether the app is on your side on the bad week. The streak counts weeks against your own target. Grace weeks are earned two-for-one and spent for you. A lapse is paused, not lost. The Consistency ladder is measured in goal-met weeks, the Grind ladder counts sessions that already happened, and the day-streak badges are gone. Notifications come in four categories you control separately, delivered in your timezone, and most of the nudging comes from people you chose.

Around it sits the rest of the app. The **Body budget** keeps a running tally of four deposits: your workout, your streak, your sleep and your nutrition, so a week where you slept well and ate properly is not scored as a zero because you trained twice. **Fuel** handles food, scan a plate or a barcode and check protein, carbs and fat, with no red numbers and no shame. The **scheduler** reshuffles a missed session into the rest of the week rather than dropping it, which is the practical version of everything argued above. The **AI coach** rebuilds a session from a sentence when the rack is taken. The programme updates week to week from what you log.

Pocket Fit exists because of the bad weeks. Georgi started at 122 kg and ended up competing at The Yard Games, having lost 38 kg on the way, and the app was built around the sessions that nearly did not happen rather than the ones that went well. That is [the whole story](https://www.pocket-fit.app/our-story), and it is why the streak forgives a week and the notification tells you what your body banked rather than what you lost.

**[Start with Pocket Fit, free](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** Set your own weekly target, earn grace weeks, and keep only the notifications you want. Personalised in minutes on [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).

## Workout accountability and streaks: common questions

### Does missing one workout really break a habit?

The best real-world evidence says no. Lally and colleagues followed 96 volunteers for 12 weeks and found that missing one opportunity to perform a behaviour did not materially affect habit formation. Automaticity rose on a curve with a median of 66 days to plateau and a range of 18 to 254 days. The curve absorbs a missed day. A daily streak counter does not, which is a fact about the counter rather than about you.

### How does a weekly workout streak work in Pocket Fit?

A week counts when your qualified sessions reach the weekly workout target you set yourself. Rest days are not events, so a three or four day a week lifter can hold a long streak while training exactly as programmed. The streak shows as active when healthy, shielded when a grace week is holding it, and paused when the chain has lapsed beyond what grace could cover.

### What is a grace week and how do I get one?

You earn one grace week for every two consecutive weeks where you hit your target, and you can hold up to three at a time. They are applied automatically to bridge an off week, so a long chain survives a holiday or an illness without you doing anything. They are derived from your session history rather than stored separately, so they always match what you actually did.

### Why did Pocket Fit remove the day-streak achievements?

Because they punished correct training. The 3, 7, 14 and 30-Day Streak badges are no longer awarded to anyone. They were replaced by a Consistency ladder counted in goal-met weeks, from First Week Solid up to 1 Year Strong, which someone training three times a week can actually climb. The old constants remain only so historically earned badges still display a name.

### Can I keep social notifications and turn the reminders off?

Yes. There are four separate categories, workout reminders, inactivity nudges, progress and achievements, and social, each with its own toggle, plus a master switch that overrides all of them. Keep friend requests and peer reactions while silencing everything else, or the reverse. All of them are sent by timezone-aware jobs, so they arrive in your local morning.

## References

4. Sirois FM, Kitner R, Hirsch JK (2015). Self-compassion, affect, and health-promoting behaviors. *Health Psychology*, 34(6), 661-669. DOI: [10.1037/hea0000158](https://doi.org/10.1037/hea0000158)
5. Rackow P, Scholz U, Hornung R (2015). Received social support and exercising: An intervention study to test the enabling hypothesis. *British Journal of Health Psychology*, 20(4), 763-776. DOI: [10.1111/bjhp.12139](https://doi.org/10.1111/bjhp.12139)

In Martin LL, Tesser A (Eds.), *Striving and Feeling: Interactions Among Goals, Affect, and Self-Regulation*, 99-120. Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. DOI: [10.4324/9781315806396](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781315806396)

blog.title: Bring your own workout: your program is probably fine, it is the blanks that break it
blog.description: The research says the structure you already chose works. What sinks it is the fields nobody writes down: rest, set count, and what weight to put on the bar today.
blog.body:
You already have a program. It is in your phone notes under a heading you typed at 11pm. It is a PDF a coach sent you in March. It is a screenshot of someone's push day, saved and never opened. It is a chat with an AI that gave you six workouts and no idea what to do with them. Or it is in your head, which is the worst filing cabinet ever built.

The reasonable assumption is that a fitness app wants to replace it. Delete your plan, take ours, start again. So you never bother, and you keep training off a screenshot, guessing the weight, resting until you feel ready, and losing the thread by week five.

The truth is that your program is very probably fine. Volume-equated research keeps failing to find the magic in one structure over another. What it does not cover is the part your notes app never captured: how long you rest, how many sets you actually do, what load you pick when you are tired, and whether anything moves up next week. Those blanks are where programs die. So Pocket Fit lets you bring yours in as it is, and fills in only what you left out.

**[Bring your own program into Pocket Fit](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** Type the workouts and the exercises, leave the rest blank, and the app resolves sets, reps, rest, loads and scheduling around your goal and your own training history. Free on [the App Store](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats), no card needed.

## What importing actually means here

The import path is deliberately small. You give a program a name, then a list of workouts. Each workout has a name and an ordered list of exercises. That is the required shape and nothing more.

Per exercise you may optionally give sets. Per set you may optionally give reps and a weight. Every one of those is allowed to be missing.

Two things are worth knowing about the mechanics. First, the whole import runs as a single database transaction, so it either lands complete or it does not land at all. You never end up with a half-built program, three workouts of a five workout week, or a Tuesday with no exercises in it. Second, nothing you type is second-guessed. If you supply a number, it is taken at face value. The app only ever fills a gap, it does not overrule you.

That distinction matters more than it sounds, and it comes back in every worked example below. A blank is filled intelligently. A wrong number is obeyed.

## The evidence: the structure you chose is probably not the problem

Here is the uncomfortable finding for anyone who has spent a fortnight choosing between programs.

*Grgic, Schoenfeld and Davies* pooled 22 studies for *Sports Medicine* on how training frequency affects strength. Raw effect sizes climbed neatly with frequency: **0.74, 0.82, 0.93 and 1.08** for one, two, three and four-plus sessions per week. A clean story, until they ran the subgroup analysis restricted to studies where total weekly volume was equated. Then the frequency effect disappeared, **p = 0.421**. Their reading is that frequency helps mainly because it lets you accumulate more quality volume, not because a muscle needs a particular number of visits.

Read that back as advice about your own plan. The upper/lower your coach wrote and the push/pull/legs you screenshotted are, at matched volume, arguing about the filing system. Which is why we wrote a [full guide to every split and how Pocket Fit builds a program from scratch](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/how-pocket-fit-builds-your-program-every-training-split-explained) - and why, if you already have a structure you like, the sensible move is to keep it.

What does show a dose response is total work. *Ralston, Kilgore, Wyatt and Baker* meta-analysed 9 studies with 223 men for *Sports Medicine* and graded weekly sets per exercise into low, medium and high bands. Effect sizes on strength were **0.82 for five sets or fewer, 0.98 for five to nine, and 1.01 for ten or more**. The curve is real but it flattens hard, and the authors are honest that only one of the nine studies was a randomised controlled design.

So: sets accumulated over weeks is the lever. The plan on your phone already lists exercises. What it almost certainly does not do is guarantee those sets happen, at a sensible load, week after week.

## The three fields nobody writes down

Open any program you have saved. Count how many of these are actually written on it.

**Rest.** Almost nobody writes rest into a notes app. You rest until the conversation ends or the phone gets boring. Pocket Fit assigns a rest timer per exercise from your goal and the exercise's role: primary compound gets 120 seconds for Gain Muscle, 180 for Gain Strength, 90 for Lose Fat. Accessory compound gets 90, 120 and 60. Isolation gets 60, 60 and 45. This is the single most commonly missing field in an imported program, and the one that quietly changes what a session is.

**Set count.** Programs written from memory drift downwards. Three sets becomes two when you are late. Pocket Fit resolves a missing set count from your goal and your experience level together, which is covered exercise by exercise below.

**Load.** This is the one with the best evidence behind it. *Steele, Malleron, Har-Nir, Androulakis-Korakakis, Wolf, Fisher and Halperin* reviewed 18 studies with 368 participants for *Sports Medicine* and asked what load people choose when left to choose. The answer: an average of **53% of one-rep max**, with a 95% credible interval of 49 to 58%. That is a long way below what most hypertrophy and strength work calls for. Left alone, we go light.

That is exactly the field an imported program leaves blank, and exactly the field the app can fill without guessing. If you do not supply a weight, Pocket Fit looks up the highest load you have historically lifted on that exact exercise, inside the prescribed rep range, from your own logged sessions, and pre-loads it. Not a percentage of an estimated max. Your actual best set at those reps.

## What you type, and what the app derives

The full fill-in logic, in one place, so the worked examples make sense.

- **Rep range** comes from your goal and the exercise's role. Gain Muscle: primary compound 6-10, accessory compound 8-12, isolation 8-12. Gain Strength: 3-6, 5-7, 8-12. Lose Fat: 6-10, 8-12, 12-15. If you gave a set but no reps, it uses the midpoint of that range.
- **Set count** comes from goal and experience level. Primary compound on Gain Muscle: 4 for Beginner, 5 for Intermediate, 4 for Advanced. Primary compound on Gain Strength: 5, 6 and 6. Accessory compound on Gain Muscle: 4, 3 and 3. Accessory compound on Gain Strength: 5, 5 and 4. Isolation is always 3.
- **Rest** as listed above, per exercise, from goal and role.
- **Weight** is taken verbatim if you give one, and converted into both kilograms and pounds so the app works in whichever units you read in. If you leave it blank, it is pulled from your own history on that exercise within that rep range.
- **Compound or isolation, primary or accessory** is not your call and not a guess. It comes from the exercise database, and it is what drives every number above.
- **Estimated calories per set and session duration** are computed for every workout. Duration is reps multiplied by 2.5 seconds, plus rest inside each exercise, plus 60 seconds between exercises, multiplied by a 1.7 real-world factor, because nobody actually moves from rack to bench in zero seconds.
- **Cover imagery** is assigned per workout, choosing unused images so your days do not all look identical in the list.
- **Scheduling** happens by default. The program is laid out on your calendar, one day slot per workout, starting tomorrow, so reminders fire without you making a second decision after you already made the hard one.

**[Keep your plan, lose the guesswork](https://www.pocket-fit.app/pricing).** Rest timers, loads and a schedule get attached to the program you already trust. Free on iOS and Android.

## Eight programs, brought in, and exactly what gets filled

### 1. The classic 5x5, three days a week

**What you type.** Program: Strength A/B. Workout A: Back Squat 5x5, Bench Press 5x5, Barbell Row 5x5. Workout B: Back Squat 5x5, Overhead Press 5x5, Deadlift 1x5. Goal Gain Strength, experience Intermediate.

**Taken verbatim.** Every set and rep. You wrote 5x5, you get 5x5. The app does not decide that an intermediate on Gain Strength "should" have 6 sets on primary compounds, because you already answered.

**Derived.** Rest of 180 seconds on squat, bench, press and deadlift, because the exercise database flags them primary compound and your goal is strength. Rest of 120 seconds on the row if it resolves as an accessory compound. Weight on each lift from your best historical set in the 3 to 6 range. Duration and calories per session. Three calendar slots from tomorrow.

**Weekly layout.** Monday A, Wednesday B, Friday A. The following week flips: Monday B, Wednesday A, Friday B.

**The field people forget.** Rest. A 5x5 done on 90 seconds is a conditioning session wearing a strength program's clothes.

### 2. A coach's four day upper/lower, fully specified

**What you type.** Four workouts, every exercise with explicit sets and reps because your coach wrote them. Upper A: Bench Press 4x6, Barbell Row 4x8, Incline Dumbbell Press 3x10, Lat Pulldown 3x10, Lateral Raise 3x15. Lower A: Back Squat 4x6, Romanian Deadlift 3x8, Leg Press 3x12, Leg Curl 3x12, Calf Raise 4x15. Then Upper B and Lower B. Goal Gain Muscle, Intermediate.

**Taken verbatim.** All of it. Sets, reps, order. This is the case where you have supplied the entire prescription.

**Derived.** Only four things, and they are the four your coach's PDF never had: rest per exercise (120 on the primary compounds, 90 on the accessory compounds, 60 on lateral raises and calves), starting loads from your history at those exact rep counts, the calendar, and progression from week two onwards.

**Weekly layout.** Monday Upper A, Tuesday Lower A, Thursday Upper B, Friday Lower B.

**The field people forget.** Again rest, and this time it is the coach who left it out. Written programs assume a coach standing there with a stopwatch. Yours is not.

### 3. A push/pull/legs screenshot with nothing but names

**What you type.** Exercise names only, copied off a screenshot. Push: Bench Press, Overhead Press, Incline Dumbbell Press, Triceps Pushdown, Lateral Raise. Pull: Deadlift, Pull-Up, Barbell Row, Face Pull, Barbell Curl. Legs: Back Squat, Romanian Deadlift, Leg Press, Leg Curl, Calf Raise. Goal Gain Muscle, Beginner.

**Taken verbatim.** The exercise list and its order. That is the whole of your contribution, and it is enough.

**Derived.** Everything else. Primary compounds get 4 sets at the midpoint of 6 to 10 reps with 120 seconds rest. Accessory compounds get 4 sets at the midpoint of 8 to 12 with 90 seconds. Isolation gets 3 sets at the midpoint of 8 to 12 with 60 seconds. Loads come from history where you have it and stay open where you do not, because inventing a bench press number for someone who has never logged one would be worse than leaving the field for you.

**Weekly layout.** Monday Push, Wednesday Pull, Friday Legs. Run it six days if you have six, and read the [beginner's guide to push/pull/legs](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/push-pull-legs-for-beginners) before you decide.

**The field people forget.** All of them. That is the point of this example: a screenshot is a list of names, and a list of names is a legitimate input.

### 4. A bro split from an old training log

**What you type.** Five workouts, one muscle group each: Chest, Back, Shoulders, Legs, Arms. You have the exercise names from a log you kept two years ago, and you also have the weights you were using back then. Goal Gain Muscle, Advanced.

**Taken verbatim.** The exercises. And, if you type them, those two-year-old weights.

**Derived.** At Advanced on Gain Muscle: 4 sets on primary compounds, 3 on accessory compounds, 3 on isolation. Rest 120, 90 and 60.

**The advice.** Leave the old weights out. This is the clearest case of the rule: a blank gets filled from what you have actually lifted recently, a typed number gets obeyed even if it is a bench press you last hit before a two-year layoff. Old logs are a good source of exercise selection and a bad source of load.

**Weekly layout.** Monday Chest, Tuesday Back, Wednesday Shoulders, Thursday Legs, Friday Arms.

**The field people forget.** Set count. Five days of one muscle group each drifts into "however many I feel like", and a bro split with no set count written down is the fastest way to a week of low volume that felt busy.

### 5. Three day full body, coming back from a layoff

**What you type.** Program: Return. Three workouts, each with five exercises: a squat pattern, a hinge, a press, a pull, one isolation. And here you type set counts on purpose, and type them low. Goblet Squat 2 sets, Romanian Deadlift 2 sets, Dumbbell Bench Press 2 sets, Seated Row 2 sets, Face Pull 2 sets. No reps. Goal Gain Muscle, Beginner.

**Taken verbatim.** Two sets. Every time. Even though a Beginner on Gain Muscle would otherwise get 4 on primary compounds and 3 on isolation.

**Derived.** Reps from the midpoint of the goal range for each role, rest of 120, 90 and 60 seconds, and loads from whatever history exists, which after a layoff is usually thin and that is fine.

**Why this example matters.** Typing a deliberately small number is how you steer the import. You are not fighting the defaults, you are using the one field that overrides them. Then let the progression engine climb from there, rather than starting at what you used to do and quitting in week two.

**Weekly layout.** Monday, Wednesday, Friday, same skeleton, different exercise choices.

### 6. A glute-focused four day from a coach

**What you type.** Lower A: Hip Thrust 4x10, Romanian Deadlift 3x10, Bulgarian Split Squat 3x12, Cable Kickback 3x15. Lower B: Back Squat 4x8, Hip Thrust 3x12, Walking Lunge 3x12, Seated Abduction 3x20. Plus two upper days. Sets and reps supplied, no weights, because the coach cannot know what you lift.

**Taken verbatim.** Sets and reps throughout.

**Derived.** The weights, and this is where the history lookup earns its place. The load pulled for the hip thrust on Lower A is your best logged hip thrust inside a 10 rep context, not your heaviest triple. The Bulgarian split squat pulls your best set at 12. Same exercise, different rep prescriptions, different starting loads, all from your own logs. Plus rest at 120, 90 and 60 seconds for a Gain Muscle goal, and a four day calendar.

**Weekly layout.** Monday Lower A, Tuesday Upper A, Thursday Lower B, Friday Upper B.

**The field people forget.** Load continuity between similar exercises. Written programs say "hip thrust 4x10" for eight weeks and never say what to put on it. Your history does.

### 7. A dumbbell-only hotel program for travel weeks

**What you type.** Program: Away. Three workouts, dumbbells only, names only: Goblet Squat, Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift, Dumbbell Bench Press, One Arm Row, Dumbbell Shoulder Press, Dumbbell Curl, Overhead Extension. Goal Gain Muscle, Intermediate.

**Taken verbatim.** The exercise names, which is precisely how you enforce the equipment constraint. You are not asking the app to guess what a hotel gym has, you are telling it.

**Derived.** Intermediate on Gain Muscle gives 5 sets on primary compounds, 3 on accessory compounds, 3 on isolation, with reps at the midpoints and rest at 120, 90 and 60. Loads come from your dumbbell history specifically, because the lookup is per exercise, so your barbell bench has no influence on your dumbbell bench.

**Weekly layout.** Three slots across the travel week, scheduled from tomorrow, so the reminders exist in the week you are least likely to organise yourself.

### 8. A rehab-constrained program with substitutions you need to keep

**What you type.** Exactly the substitutions your physiotherapist agreed. Trap Bar Deadlift instead of conventional. Goblet Squat instead of back squat. Floor Press instead of bench. Neutral Grip Pulldown instead of wide grip. Sets and reps where you have them, blank where you do not.

**Taken verbatim.** The exercise list, unconditionally. The import never swaps an exercise for something it thinks is better. What you typed is what you train.

**Derived.** Rest, any missing set counts and rep targets, loads from history on those specific movements, and the schedule.

**Why this is the strongest case for importing.** A generated program works from a limitations field written in plain language, which is a good system. But if you have been told precisely what to do and what to avoid, typing the list yourself removes the interpretation step entirely.

**[Import the plan you were actually given](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).** Substitutions stay exactly as written, and only the blanks get filled. Free on iOS and Android.

## How to clean up a program in your notes before you bring it in

Five minutes of tidying makes the difference between an import that lands and an argument with your own handwriting.

- **One workout per heading.** "Upper A", "Lower A", "Push". If your notes have one long list, split it first. The import is a list of workouts, and the workout names become the names you see in the app.
- **One exercise per line.** No paragraphs, no "then some arms". A line is an exercise.
- **Sets and reps if you have them, in the order you will train them.** Write "4x8" or "4 sets of 8". Leave the whole thing off if you are not sure.
- **Weight only if it is current.** A weight you hit last week is useful. A weight from a different bodyweight and a different year is a trap.
- **Delete anything you are unsure about.** This is the whole rule in one line: a blank gets filled intelligently, a wrong number gets taken at face value. Uncertainty is better expressed as an empty field than as a guess.

If you have a coach's PDF or a screenshot, read it and retype the lines. It is genuinely faster than it sounds, and it forces you to notice the exercise you have been skipping for a month.

## Where the AI assistant helps, and where it does not

Pocket Fit has an in-app assistant, a virtual PT backed by a retrieval index over fitness knowledge. It is useful while you are getting a program in: ask what a Bulgarian split squat is loading, ask for a substitution when the hotel gym has no cable stack, ask whether an exercise belongs early or late in a session, ask what a movement pattern is for.

It is not a document scanner, and this post is not going to pretend otherwise. The honest workflow is: get the program out of the PDF, the screenshot, the chat or your head, into a clean list of workouts and exercises. Bring that in. Let the app fill the rest. The assistant helps at the first step and the app handles the third. Anything more than that would be a claim, not a feature.

## Where the evidence runs thin

The case for importing your own plan rests on volume-equated research and on autoregulation, and both have real holes.

*Zhang and colleagues* meta-analysed 8 studies with 166 athletes for *Frontiers in Physiology* and found autoregulated loading beat fixed loading with an overall effect size of **0.64 (95% CI 0.43 to 0.85)**, worth about 4.65 kg on squat one-rep max and 3.21 kg on bench. Encouraging. But 151 of the 166 subjects were male, only 29.5% had a directly tested 1RM, every intervention ran 5 to 10 weeks, and everyone was already an athlete.

Then the counterweight. *Hickmott, Chilibeck, Shaw and Butcher* reviewed 15 studies with 441 participants for *Sports Medicine - Open* and found **no significant difference between autoregulated and standardised load prescription for 1RM strength (mean difference 2.07 kg, p = 0.09)**. Repetitions-in-reserve based autoregulation nudged towards a benefit, standardised mean difference 0.30 at p = 0.06, which is a shrug rather than a finding. Only one included study measured hypertrophy at all, so no meta-analysis on muscle size was possible.

Two reviews, opposite conclusions, same broad question. That should lower your confidence in anyone claiming loading method is decisive. It should also lower your confidence in the folklore that a program must be followed to the percentage point.

Add the rest of the caveats. Ralston's dose-response ran on 223 men, one randomised design out of nine, and the authors flag that repeated 1RM testing may itself explain some of the gains. Grgic's volume-equated null is a subgroup analysis, which is weaker evidence than a trial designed to test it. Steele's 53% figure comes from mostly short, acute studies, not from people running their own program for a year. And none of this research tested imported programs in an app, because nobody has studied that. What it supports is a modest claim: your structure is unlikely to be the limiting factor, and consistent loading and progression are worth more than the label on the split.

## Put it together

Your program is a list of exercises with holes in it. The holes are rest, set count, load and a date in the calendar, and those holes are where adherence leaks out.

Pocket Fit's import closes them without touching what you decided. Sets and reps you supply are obeyed. Sets and reps you leave out come from your goal and your experience level. Rest comes from the goal and the exercise's role, which is read from the exercise database rather than guessed. Weights you leave blank come from the heaviest you have actually lifted on that exercise in that rep range. The programme is scheduled from tomorrow, one slot per workout, with session duration and calories estimated so you know what you are agreeing to. The whole thing lands as one transaction or not at all.

After that, progression is handled by the same deterministic engine that runs generated programs. Reps climb first, up to a cap of 15, and weight only moves after a confirmed plateau where two sessions match. That is covered properly in [reps first, then weight](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/progressive-overload-reps-first-then-weight), and it is the difference between a program you imported and a program that is still going in March.

Around it sits the rest of the app: the **Body budget** tallying your workout, your streak, your sleep and your nutrition; **Fuel** for logging food by scanning a plate or a barcode, with no red numbers and no shame; the **scheduler**, which reshuffles a missed session into the rest of the week instead of dropping it; and the **AI coach**, which rebuilds a session from a sentence when the squat rack is taken. The programme updates week to week from what you log.

Pocket Fit exists because of the bad weeks. Georgi started at 122 kg and ended up competing at The Yard Games, having lost 38 kg on the way, and the app was built around the sessions that nearly did not happen rather than the ones that went well. That is [the whole story](https://www.pocket-fit.app/our-story), and it is why the import path assumes you already had a plan and lost the thread, not that you never had one.

**[Start with Pocket Fit, free](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** Bring the program you already have, leave the blanks blank, and let it fill them from your own history. Personalised in minutes on [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).

## Importing your own workout: common questions

### Can I import a workout program from a PDF or a screenshot?

Not as a file. The workflow is to read the PDF or screenshot and retype it as a list of workouts and exercises, which for a typical four day program takes a few minutes. Sets, reps and weights are optional on every line, so you only type what you actually know.

### What happens if I only know the exercise names?

That is a complete input. Set count is resolved from your goal and experience level, for example 4 sets on a primary compound for a Beginner on Gain Muscle and 5 for an Intermediate. Reps come from the midpoint of the goal's range for that exercise role, 6-10 for a primary compound on Gain Muscle. Rest comes from the same matrix, 120 seconds in that case.

### Where do the starting weights come from?

From your own logged sessions. For any set you leave without a weight, the app looks up the heaviest load you have lifted on that exact exercise inside the prescribed rep range and pre-loads it. If you supply a weight it is used as given and converted into both kilograms and pounds. With no history on an exercise, the field stays open rather than being invented.

### Will the app change my exercises?

No. The exercise list you type is taken verbatim, including deliberate substitutions such as a floor press instead of a bench press or a trap bar deadlift instead of conventional. The app only classifies each exercise as compound or isolation, primary or accessory, which is what drives sets, reps and rest.

### Does an imported program progress like a generated one?

Yes, it runs on the same engine. Reps increase first, up to a cap of 15, and the weight only moves once a plateau is confirmed by two matching sessions. Scheduling is also automatic, one day slot per workout starting tomorrow, so reminders work without a second decision.
