blog.title: The best workout split is the one your week survives: how Pocket Fit builds your program, and every split it offers
blog.description: The split you pick matters far less than how often each muscle gets trained, and that single number, per muscle weekly frequency, is decided the moment you choose your training days.
blog.body:
You open the app, you are asked for a split, and you freeze. Push/Pull/Legs sounds serious. The bro split sounds like something you should have grown out of. Full body sounds like what beginners do. So you pick the one with the best reputation, and three weeks later you are missing the fourth day, then the third, and the program quietly stops being a program.

The reasonable assumption is that the split is the plan. It is not. The split is a filing system. It decides which muscles share a day, and almost nothing else. What actually drives the result is how many times each muscle gets trained in a week and how many hard sets it collects, and those two numbers are set by your training days, not by the label on the split.

Once you see it that way, choosing gets easy, and a few of the rules Pocket Fit enforces stop looking arbitrary. This is the full guide: how a program is generated inside the app, then every split it offers, with the weekly layout, the frequency maths, the trade-offs, and the person who should not pick it.

**[Build your split with Pocket Fit](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** Tell it your goal, your days and the equipment you actually have, and it writes the week around them instead of handing you a template. Free on [the App Store](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats), no card needed.

## What the app asks you, and why each answer changes the program

Generation takes six required inputs and a few optional ones. None of them are cosmetic.

- **Goal.** Gain Muscle, Lose Fat, or Gain Strength. This sets the rep ranges, the set counts and the rest periods, and nothing else in the app rewrites them afterwards.
- **Days per week.** The single most consequential answer. It sets per muscle frequency and it constrains which splits are even legal.
- **Difficulty.** Beginner, Intermediate or Advanced. This changes set counts and it changes which exercises are eligible, because the exercise database is filtered by a difficulty-based frequency tier before anything is picked.
- **Equipment.** Bodyweight, Dumbbells, Barbells, Machines, Cables. Any combination. This is a hard filter, so nothing gets prescribed that you cannot physically perform.
- **Split.** One of seven, covered in detail below.
- **Limitations and injuries.** Free text. Written in plain language, "dodgy left shoulder", "no overhead pressing", "bad knees on deep flexion".
- **Optional: exercise variations** and a **duration in weeks**.

The output is not immediately your program. It is created as an artifact: a full draft week you can read, swap exercises inside, or reject entirely and regenerate. Only when you accept it does it become the thing you train. That gap matters, because a generated plan you never inspected is a plan you never agreed to.

## How the program is actually generated, step by step

This is the part most apps do not describe, so here it is plainly.

**Step one: your inputs become a query.** Goal, days, difficulty, equipment and split are turned into an embedding and used to search a vector index of program templates. What comes back is not a workout. It is a skeleton.

**Step two: the template is a set of slots, not exercises.** Each training day arrives as a workout structure string: a sequence of exercise slots separated by arrows, plus per-day exercise rules, target areas, and a written explanation of what the day is for. A slot is a role, for example a primary compound for a given target area, an accessory compound, or an isolation movement. The system reads the focus words out of the slot and flags it as primary, compound or accessory.

**Step three: the candidate list is pre-filtered before anything chooses.** For each slot, the exercise database is narrowed by difficulty-based frequency, preferring high frequency movements first and dropping to medium frequency next, then by your actual equipment, then by the target area for that slot. Only after that narrowing does a model pick the single best exercise from what remains, and it writes a one sentence reason for the pick. That sentence is the comment you see next to the exercise in the app. The model is choosing from a legal shortlist, not inventing from the whole world of exercises.

**Step four: anti-repetition rules run twice.** Within a workout: no two exercises from the same movement family, only one deadlift variation, only one row variation, a balance between dumbbell and barbell pressing, and no doubling up on the same isolation pattern such as two triceps extensions. Across the week: a running avoid list carries forward so the same movement does not appear on every day.

**Step five: nothing is allowed to fail.** If a slot filter returns an empty list, the selection relaxes through four progressive fallback tiers rather than producing a hole in your week. A slightly less ideal exercise beats a missing one.

**Step six: prescription and scheduling.** Sets, reps and rest are applied from a fixed matrix, given below. Session duration and calories are estimated. The program is scheduled by default, so reminders work without you making a second decision after you already made the hard one.

## Per muscle weekly frequency: the number that actually decides the outcome

Before the splits, the maths that makes them comparable. Take any split and ask one question: how many times per week does a given muscle get trained?

- Full body, 3 days: roughly **3 times per week** per muscle.
- Upper/Lower plus a full body day, 3 to 5 days: **2 to 3 times**.
- Push/Pull/Legs, 6 days: **2 times**. At 3 days: **once**.
- Upper duo split, 4 days: **once to twice**, depending on the muscle.
- Bro split, 5 days: **once**.
- Glute focused, 4 days: glutes and hamstrings **2 to 3 times**, upper body once or twice.

That number is not trivia. *Schoenfeld, Ogborn and Krieger* pooled ten studies for *Sports Medicine* and compared training a muscle once a week against twice a week with total weekly volume held equal. Twice a week produced a **larger effect on muscle size, 0.49 against 0.30**. Same sets, better result, purely from spreading them.

Their controlled trial in the *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research* pushed the same idea in trained men. Twenty well-trained lifters were randomised to a once-a-week split routine or three full body sessions a week, with volume equated across eight weeks. Strength gains were similar. Muscle thickness favoured the higher frequency group, significantly so in the forearm flexors.

Now the honest counterweight, because it changes the advice. *Grgic and colleagues* pooled 22 studies in *Sports Medicine* on frequency and strength. Raw effect sizes rose with frequency, **0.74, 0.82, 0.93 and 1.08 for one, two, three and four-plus sessions per week**. But in the volume-equated subgroup analysis the frequency effect vanished. Their reading: frequency mostly helps because it lets you accumulate more good volume, not because the muscle needs a specific number of touches.

That is the whole argument in one line. Frequency is a delivery mechanism for volume. Pick the split that lets you deliver more quality sets in the days you genuinely have.

**[Let Pocket Fit do the frequency maths](https://www.pocket-fit.app/pricing).** You choose the days you can train, it chooses the structure that fits them. Free on iOS and Android.

## Full Body Split: 1 to 6 days a week

**Who it is for.** Beginners, anyone training two or three days a week, anyone with an unpredictable schedule, and returning lifters rebuilding a habit. It is also, quietly, the strongest option for a lot of experienced lifters who only have three sessions in them.

**The weekly layout.** Every session touches a lower body push, a lower body hinge, an upper body push, an upper body pull, and one or two isolation slots. Three days looks like Monday, Wednesday, Friday, each a complete session with different exercise choices for the same patterns.

**The fatigue and frequency logic.** At three days you hit every muscle three times a week with a comfortable rest day between sessions. Volume per muscle per session is low, which is exactly why it recovers. You are trading depth per session for repetition across the week.

**Per muscle weekly frequency.** 2 days: 2 times. 3 days: 3 times. 4 or more days: 4 or more, which is where it starts to strain.

**Trade-offs.** Sessions run long if you try to give every muscle a fair share. Per-muscle volume in a single session is capped by fatigue, because by the time you reach your fifth pattern you are not fresh. And it is hard to specialise. If your rear delts are lagging, full body gives you one slot to fix it, three times a week, not four slots once a week.

**Common failure modes.** Squatting heavy at the start of every session and eventually dreading the session. Running the same five exercises for twelve weeks, which is a boredom problem more than a physiology one. And bloating the session to nine exercises so it takes ninety minutes and stops fitting your evening.

**Who should not pick it.** Anyone who genuinely trains five or six days and wants high per-muscle volume. Anyone training around an injury that makes one pattern painful, because in full body that pattern shows up every session. And anyone whose bottleneck is arm or shoulder detail work, which needs slots that a full body day does not have.

## Upper/Lower + Full Body: 1 to 6 days a week

**Who it is for.** The intermediate who has three or four days and does not want to choose between the frequency of full body and the volume of a split. This is the most flexible structure the app offers, and for most people asking "what is the best workout split", it is the honest answer.

**The weekly layout.** At four days: Upper, Lower, Upper, Lower. At three days: Upper, Lower, Full Body. At five: Upper, Lower, Upper, Lower, Full Body. The full body day acts as a patch, catching whatever the week under-served.

**The fatigue and frequency logic.** Splitting upper from lower halves the fatigue that competes inside a session. You can press hard without your squat already having drained you. The full body day then restores frequency without adding a fifth or sixth training day.

**Per muscle weekly frequency.** 3 days: 2 times. 4 days: 2 times. 5 days: 2 to 3 times.

**Trade-offs.** Lower days are brutal if you program them honestly, because squat, hinge and single-leg work in one session is a lot of systemic fatigue. Upper days can feel scattered, since chest, back, shoulders and arms all want attention in one sitting.

**Common failure modes.** Treating the full body day as an optional extra and skipping it, which turns a 2 to 3 times structure into a 2 times structure and quietly removes the whole reason you picked it. Also, front-loading both upper days with pressing and letting back volume drift below pressing volume.

**Who should not pick it.** Someone training exactly six days who wants clean, repeatable sessions, because at six days this structure gets fiddly and Push/Pull/Legs is simply tidier. And a raw beginner who would be better served by the simplicity of full body.

## Push/Pull/Legs: 3 or 6 days a week only

**Who it is for.** Intermediates and above who can commit to either three or six sessions, no other number. The three day version is far more beginner-friendly than its reputation suggests, as covered in the [push pull legs guide for beginners](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/push-pull-legs-for-beginners).

**The weekly layout.** Push covers chest, shoulders and triceps. Pull covers back and biceps. Legs covers quads, hamstrings, glutes and calves. Three days: one rotation. Six days: two rotations, usually with the second rotation biased differently, more incline and more horizontal rowing, or more hinge and less squat.

**The fatigue and frequency logic.** This is the cleanest fatigue segregation available. Muscles that already assist each other train together, so nothing gets hit twice by accident. Your triceps are not fried on pull day, and your lower back is not the limiting factor on push day.

**Per muscle weekly frequency.** 3 days: once. 6 days: twice.

**Why only 3 or 6.** Because the rotation has three elements and only divides evenly into three and six. At four days you would be running Push, Pull, Legs, Push, and next week starts on Pull, so your weeks stop repeating, your frequency becomes uneven across muscles, and any weekly comparison you try to make is noise. At five days it is worse. The app refuses those numbers rather than shipping a rotation that drifts out of phase with your calendar.

**Trade-offs.** The three day version gives every muscle only one weekly session, which is the lower-frequency arm of the Schoenfeld data. The six day version is a real commitment, and six sessions of mediocre effort is not superior to four sessions of hard effort.

**Common failure modes.** Choosing six days aspirationally, hitting four, and ending up with lopsided frequency where legs get trained once and chest three times. Also, push day becoming a chest day with a token shoulder movement bolted on at the end when you are already empty.

**Who should not pick it.** Anyone whose real availability is four or five days. That is not a soft preference, the validator will reject it. Pick Upper/Lower + Full Body at four or five days instead.

## The Upper Duo Split, Back & Biceps / Chest & Triceps / Shoulders / Legs: 4 days only

**Who it is for.** The lifter with exactly four days who wants upper body detail and does not want to hide shoulders at the end of a push day. It is a bodybuilding structure with a physique bias.

**The weekly layout.** Day one back and biceps. Day two chest and triceps. Day three shoulders, which gets a full session of its own. Day four legs.

**The fatigue and frequency logic.** Pairing a large muscle with the small muscle that already assists it is the same logic as PPL, but the shoulders are pulled out into their own day. The result is that lateral and rear delts get real volume instead of the two exhausted sets they usually receive.

**Per muscle weekly frequency.** Once for most muscles. Biceps and triceps get a little indirect work on their partner day, so they are effectively somewhere between one and two.

**Why only 4.** The rotation has exactly four named days. There is no coherent fifth day and no way to compress it into three without merging two of the four and losing the point of the structure.

**Trade-offs.** One leg day a week. That is the cost, and for a lifter who cares about lower body strength it is a real one. Everything is on a once-a-week frequency, which is the weaker side of the frequency evidence, and the compensation has to be volume within the session.

**Common failure modes.** The leg day becoming the one that gets skipped, which turns this into a four day upper body program. And under-training the rear delts even on a dedicated shoulder day, because pressing feels more productive than raises.

**Who should not pick it.** Anyone chasing lower body strength or glute development. Anyone who cannot train four days, since the validator accepts nothing else. And anyone in their first six months, who would build more from higher frequency on the basics.

## The Bro Split, one muscle group per day: 5 days only

**Who it is for.** The lifter who trains five days, enjoys long focused sessions, and wants to hammer one area at a time. It has a bad reputation online and a very good adherence record in real gyms, which is worth saying out loud.

**The weekly layout.** Chest, back, shoulders, arms, legs. Five days, five slots, one muscle group each.

**The fatigue and frequency logic.** Maximum local volume, maximum recovery time. Your chest gets fifteen to twenty hard sets and then a full week to recover. The bet is that a large single dose plus long recovery matches smaller doses spread out.

**Per muscle weekly frequency.** Once. There is no version of this split that changes that.

**Why only 5.** The structure needs exactly five slots to cover chest, back, shoulders, arms and legs. Drop to four and one of them disappears. Go to six and you are repeating a day, which is no longer a bro split.

**Trade-offs.** This is the structure most at odds with the frequency data. *Schoenfeld's* meta-analysis put once weekly at an effect of 0.30 against 0.49 for twice weekly at equal volume, and the eight week trial in well-trained men favoured the higher frequency group on muscle thickness. The counter-argument is *Grgic's* finding that the frequency advantage disappears once volume is equated, which means a bro split can work if the weekly volume genuinely lands. The problem is that fifteen sets for one muscle in one session are not fifteen equal sets. Set twelve is not set two.

**Common failure modes.** Missing chest day and going eleven days between chest sessions, because there is no second touch to absorb the miss. Junk volume in the back half of the session. And arms day becoming the favourite while legs day becomes the one with a plausible excuse.

**Who should not pick it.** Beginners, who get more from frequency and from practising each movement more often. Anyone with an unreliable schedule, since one missed day costs a full week of that muscle. And anyone training three or four days, where it is not even offered.

## The Glute-Focused Split: 1 to 6 days a week

**Who it is for.** Anyone whose priority is glutes and posterior chain, most commonly women training three to five days, but the structure is available to anyone who selects it.

**The weekly layout.** Lower body days built around hip thrusts, Romanian deadlifts, split squats, step-ups and reverse lunges, with upper body days interleaved so the lower body gets recovery without you losing training days.

**The fatigue and frequency logic.** Glutes tolerate frequency well, partly because hip thrust and bridge patterns generate far less systemic fatigue than heavy squatting for the same amount of glute work. That is why this split can put glutes on two or three days a week without wrecking you, where three heavy squat sessions would.

**Per muscle weekly frequency.** Glutes and hamstrings 2 to 3 times. Quads 1 to 2. Upper body 1 to 2.

**Trade-offs.** Upper body volume is lower by design. If you want a balanced physique you will need to accept slower upper body progress, or add days.

**Common failure modes.** Piling on more and more glute isolation while the loaded hinge and thrust barely progress, which is volume standing in for overload. The fix is the same rule as everywhere else: earn the reps first, then add the weight, as laid out in the [reps first then weight guide](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/progressive-overload-reps-first-then-weight).

**Who should not pick it.** Anyone whose main goal is upper body size or general strength. Anyone with a hip or lower back issue that makes repeated hinging painful, at least until that is assessed properly.

## BodyWeight Push / Pull / Legs, and the override you did not ask for

**Who it is for.** Home trainers, travellers, and anyone who selected Bodyweight and nothing else.

**The override.** If the only equipment you select is Bodyweight, the app overrides whatever split you chose and gives you the bodyweight Push/Pull/Legs structure. That is a deliberate decision, and it is worth defending.

A bro split without external load falls apart. A dedicated shoulder day with no dumbbells is push-ups on an angle and holds, which is a push day wearing a different name. A dedicated arms day with no weights is close-grip push-ups and inverted rows, which is push and pull. Once you remove external load, the only thing that meaningfully separates a training day is the movement pattern, so the app collapses to the structure that is defined by movement pattern: push, pull, legs. Every other split would be a label over the same three sessions, and a label that promises something it cannot deliver is worse than no label.

**The weekly layout.** Push: push-up variations, dips, pike push-ups. Pull: rows, chin-ups, whatever anchor you have. Legs: squat, split squat, hinge and single-leg variations, with tempo and range doing the work that load normally does.

**Per muscle weekly frequency.** Once at three days, twice at six.

**Trade-offs.** Pulling is the honest weak point. Without a bar or rings, back training is genuinely limited, and no amount of programming fixes that. Progression relies on leverage, tempo, range and unilateral variations rather than the bar going up, which is slower to measure.

**Who should not pick it.** Nobody, if bodyweight is all you have. But if you own a single pair of dumbbells, select them. It changes the program more than any other input you can give.

## The prescription matrix, exactly as the app applies it

Once the exercises are chosen, sets, reps and rest come from a fixed matrix keyed on your goal, your experience and the slot type. These are the actual numbers.

**Rep ranges by goal and slot type**

| Slot | Gain Muscle | Gain Strength | Lose Fat |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary compound | 6 to 10 | 3 to 6 | 6 to 10 |
| Accessory compound | 8 to 12 | 5 to 7 | 8 to 12 |
| Isolation | 8 to 12 | 8 to 12 | 12 to 15 |

**Sets by goal, experience and slot type**

| Slot and goal | Beginner | Intermediate | Advanced |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary compound, Gain Muscle | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| Primary compound, Gain Strength | 5 | 6 | 6 |
| Primary compound, Lose Fat | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| Accessory compound, Gain Muscle | 4 | 3 | 3 |
| Accessory compound, Gain Strength | 5 | 5 | 4 |
| Accessory compound, Lose Fat | 4 | 3 | 3 |
| Isolation, any goal | 3 | 3 | 3 |

**Rest in seconds by goal and slot type**

| Slot | Gain Muscle | Gain Strength | Lose Fat |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary compound | 120 | 180 | 90 |
| Accessory compound | 90 | 120 | 60 |
| Isolation | 60 | 60 | 45 |

Two things worth noticing. Advanced lifters do not always get more sets than intermediates, because at that level per-session volume is often reduced in favour of quality and recovery. And rest is treated as part of the prescription, not an afterthought, because 180 seconds on a heavy triple and 45 seconds on a fat loss isolation set are doing completely different jobs.

Session length is estimated rather than guessed: reps multiplied by 2.5 seconds per rep, plus all the intra-exercise rest, plus 60 seconds between exercises, then multiplied by a 1.7 real world factor. That factor is a 1.5 base plus a 20 percent contingency, because people do not train at stopwatch pace. They re-rack, they wait for the bench, they check a message. An estimate that assumes otherwise is a lie that makes you late.

Calories per set are estimated too, which feeds the [training and deficit relationship](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/calorie-deficit-and-strength-training) rather than pretending training and eating are separate problems.

**[Get the numbers written for you](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).** Sets, reps and rest arrive already matched to your goal and experience, and they update as you log. Free on iOS and Android.

## The rules that differ for female users

The generator applies a distinct selection rule set for female users. This is not a softer program, it is a different set of defaults, and it is worth stating plainly rather than hiding.

- Lower body selection is glute-priority: hip thrusts, Romanian deadlifts, split squats, step-ups and reverse lunges are preferred.
- Barbell is preferred for hip thrust and Romanian deadlift patterns, because those are the two lifts where load matters most for the target.
- Barbell and smith machine bench press patterns are excluded, with dumbbell or machine chest press used instead.
- Flies are dumbbell or machine rather than cable crossovers.
- Triceps work uses rope and cable pushdowns rather than overhead cable extensions.
- Curls are dumbbell rather than machine.
- Only one squat variation and one bridge variation per workout, strictly enforced.

You can override any of it inside the artifact before you accept the program. The defaults reflect what most users in that group actually want and stick with. They are not a claim about what anyone is capable of.

## Where the evidence runs thin

Everything above sits on a body of research with real limits, and you should know them.

The frequency meta-analysis by *Schoenfeld and colleagues* rests on ten studies, most of them short, most of them in untrained or moderately trained participants, and mostly in men. The controlled trial that supports it had **twenty participants across eight weeks**, which is a small sample over a short window for a question about long-term physique change.

*Grgic's* volume-equated finding is the one that should make you cautious about strong split claims. When volume was matched, frequency stopped mattering for strength. That directly undercuts the simple story that more frequent is better.

*Ralston and colleagues* pooled 9 studies and 61 treatment groups for *Sports Medicine* and found a graded dose-response for weekly sets, but the effect sizes were modest, **0.23 for high versus low weekly sets on isolation exercises and 0.15 for medium versus low**. Real, but not dramatic.

*Schoenfeld, Ogborn and Krieger's* volume dose-response in the *Journal of Sports Sciences* found each additional weekly set worth an effect of **0.023, about 0.37 percent extra gain**. Also real. Also small, and it does not continue upward forever.

And none of these studies compared named splits. There is no trial of "Push/Pull/Legs versus bro split". Researchers manipulate frequency and volume, not marketing labels, so every split argument in this article is an inference from frequency and volume data, not a direct test. Anyone telling you a specific split is proven superior is going past the evidence.

The practical conclusion is deflating and useful: within sensible ranges, the split you pick matters much less than whether you complete it.

## How to actually choose, in four questions

1. **How many days will you train in your worst week, not your best?** Answer honestly and pick from what is legal at that number. Two or three days: full body. Four or five: Upper/Lower + Full Body. Exactly three or six: Push/Pull/Legs is on the table. Exactly four with an upper body bias: upper duo. Exactly five with long sessions you enjoy: bro split.
2. **What is your equipment, really?** If it is bodyweight only, the choice is made for you, and correctly. If you can add one thing, add dumbbells.
3. **What is the goal for the next twelve weeks?** Gain Strength buys you 3 to 6 rep primaries and 180 second rests. Lose Fat buys 12 to 15 rep isolation and 45 second rests. They are different programs, not the same program with different food.
4. **What have you actually finished before?** If you have never completed eight weeks of anything, take the structure with the fewest ways to fail.

## Put it together

The split is a filing system. Frequency is the delivery mechanism. Volume is the dose. Adherence is the multiplier that sits in front of all three, and if it is zero, nothing behind it matters.

Pocket Fit is built around that order. It asks for your real days first and constrains the split to what divides evenly into them, which is why Push/Pull/Legs takes only 3 or 6, the upper duo only 4, and the bro split only 5. It filters exercises to the equipment you have before anything gets chosen, so nothing in your week is theoretical. It applies the same prescription matrix every time, so your sets and rests are not a mood. Once the program exists, the deterministic progression engine takes over: reps first to a cap of fifteen, weight only off a confirmed plateau, which is the [reps first then weight rule](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/progressive-overload-reps-first-then-weight) doing the boring work in the background. The **scheduler** reshuffles a missed session into the rest of the week instead of dropping it, which is the single most valuable thing you can do to a bro split. The **AI coach** rebuilds a session from a sentence when your shoulder is angry or the squat rack is taken. **Fuel** keeps the food side in the same place, scan a plate or a barcode, check protein, carbs and fat, no red numbers and no shame. And the **Body budget** keeps a running tally of the four deposits, your workout, your streak, your sleep and your nutrition, so a hard week reads as a hard week rather than a failure.

Georgi built it because of the bad weeks, not the good ones. He went from 122 kg to competing at The Yard Games, having lost 38 kg along the way, and the version of him who kept missing the fourth day is the person every one of those rules is written for. That story is at [our story](https://www.pocket-fit.app/our-story).

**[Start with Pocket Fit, free](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** Answer six questions, read the draft week, change what you want, then train it. Personalised in minutes on [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).

## Workout splits: common questions

### What is the best workout split for building muscle?

The one that lets each muscle be trained at least twice a week within the days you will genuinely complete. *Schoenfeld's* meta-analysis found twice weekly beat once weekly at equal volume, an effect of 0.49 against 0.30. For most people that means full body at two or three days, Upper/Lower + Full Body at four or five, or Push/Pull/Legs at six.

### Why does Pocket Fit only allow 3 or 6 days for Push/Pull/Legs?

Because the rotation has three elements and only divides evenly into three and six. At four or five days the rotation drifts out of phase with the calendar, so your weeks stop repeating and each muscle gets a different frequency from week to week. The validator rejects those numbers instead of shipping a structure that quietly stops working.

### Is the bro split really that bad?

Not bad, just the least forgiving. It trains each muscle once a week, which is the weaker arm of the frequency evidence, and one missed session means eleven days without that muscle. *Grgic's* volume-equated analysis found that frequency stopped mattering once volume was matched, so a bro split can work, provided the weekly sets genuinely land and the last third of each session is not junk.

### How many sets per muscle per week should I do?

More helps, with diminishing returns. *Ralston* found effects of 0.23 for high versus low weekly sets and 0.15 for medium versus low, and *Schoenfeld* found each extra weekly set worth about 0.37 percent additional gain. Those are real but modest numbers, which is why the app prescribes 3 to 6 sets per slot rather than chasing volume for its own sake.

### Why did the app change my split to bodyweight?

Because Bodyweight was the only equipment you selected. Without external load, the only thing that meaningfully separates one training day from another is the movement pattern, so a bro split or an upper duo split would just be push, pull and legs under a misleading name. Select dumbbells if you have them, and the full set of splits opens back up.

blog.title: The Progressive Overload Variable Nobody Logs: How Sleep Decides When the Weight Goes Up
blog.description: A stalled bar is usually read as a programming failure. More often it is a recovery signal, and the input driving it is the one thing you never write down: your sleep.
blog.body:
You have run the same lift for three weeks and the number on the bar has not moved. So you do what everyone does. You question the programme. Maybe the rep range is wrong. Maybe you need more volume, a different split, a deload, a new app. "Why am I not progressing in the gym" is one of the most searched training questions there is, and almost every answer points at your programming.

Here is the part that gets missed. A good progression engine does not add weight because a calendar says so. It adds weight because you earned it, by grinding out an extra rep and then holding it. And the thing that quietly decides whether you can earn those reps is not in your training log at all. It is how you slept last week.

The truth is a little strange. On paper your plan is fine. What stalled was the input nobody records.

**[Train smarter with Pocket Fit](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** It watches your reps climb and only moves the weight when you have genuinely earned it. Free on [the App Store](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats), no card needed.

## How progression is actually earned

Progressive overload sounds like a plan to add weight every week. In a sane system it is not. Weight is the reward at the end of a queue, and reps come first.

Pocket Fit's progression engine works in a strict order. It only ever raises your reps, one at a time, up to a cap of fifteen. You do not jump the weight because a week has passed. You add a rep, then another, and the load stays put while you climb. Only once the reps stop climbing, and stay stopped, does the weight become the next move.

And it will not move the weight on a single flat session. It waits for a confirmed plateau, the same reps at the same load across two sessions in a row, before it nudges the bar up in a small, honest step. On a barbell that is +5 kg for men and +2.5 kg for women. On dumbbells, cables and most other kit it is smaller still, +2.5 kg or +1.25 kg. Tiny, deliberate, earned.

This is covered in more depth in [reps first, then weight](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/progressive-overload-reps-first-then-weight). The point that matters here is the machinery. The engine needs a signal to fire. That signal is you completing more reps than last time, or clearly holding a plateau. No signal, no progression. And the fastest way to starve the engine of that signal is to keep turning up under-slept.

## What short sleep does to the reps

Miss enough sleep and the session does not collapse. It just gets heavier in your hands, and the reps quietly stop coming.

Craven and colleagues pooled the field for *Sports Medicine* in 2022, a systematic review and meta-analysis of 69 studies and 227 performance measures. Across everything, acute sleep loss dropped physical performance by a mean of **7.56%**. That is not a wipeout. It is exactly the kind of margin that turns a set of eight into a set of seven. The weight you would have progressed past now feels like the weight you can barely hold, and the extra rep the engine was waiting for never lands.

The reason it feels harder matters. Sleep loss barely dents your absolute strength on a fresh single, but it raises how hard a given load feels and it drags down repeated output. So on paper your one-rep max is intact, yet your working sets, the sets that actually earn reps, come up short. The [session feels harder while the number barely moves](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/training-on-poor-sleep-what-to-change-and-what-to-keep), and it is the working sets that feed the progression engine.

Underneath that, the building slows too. Saner and colleagues put 24 young men through five nights of four-hour sleep and measured muscle protein synthesis, the rate at which muscle is actually built. Sleep restriction pushed it **down by roughly 18%**. A run of short nights does not just make today's session feel worse. It slows the recovery that was meant to turn this week's work into next week's rep.

So the plateau you are staring at is doing exactly what a plateau should. It is telling the engine you have not earned the next step yet. It is just that the reason is sitting in your sleep, not your spreadsheet.

## Consistency beats chasing one big night

The instinct after a bad week is to bank a huge lie-in and call it fixed. The evidence points somewhere duller and more useful: it is the run of nights that moves the needle, not any single hero sleep.

Craven's numbers came from repeated and acute sleep loss, and Saner's effect came from five consecutive short nights, not one. That is the shape of the problem. A single rough night barely registers in your training. It is the fourth and fifth short night in a row that quietly pulls your working sets down and stalls the reps. Which means the fix is not one perfect night either. It is protecting a steady floor across the week so the reps keep coming and the engine keeps getting its signal.

This is why [sleep consistency, not just duration, tracks with the weight on the bar](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/sleep-consistency-and-the-weight-on-the-bar). Chasing an extra hour once is a rounding error. Holding a boring, repeatable bedtime is what keeps you earning reps.

## What to do on a badly slept week

You do not need to skip. You need to protect the reps you can still bank so the plateau stays honest and does not turn into a slide. On a rough week:

- **Keep the session.** A bad night is not a rest day. Turning up defends the muscle you have; in Saner's study the group who exercised held their build rate at baseline despite the sleep loss.
- **Go a touch lighter, keep the reps clean.** Shave the load slightly and hit your rep targets with good bars rather than grinding failures. Clean reps are the signal the engine wants.
- **Protect the top sets.** If anything gets cut, cut junk volume, not the working sets that actually earn progression.
- **Do not force the weight.** A missed rep target under-slept is real information. Let the plateau stand rather than muscling the bar up on a bad week.

Do this and you come out the far side of the week with your reps intact and the engine still armed, instead of a stall you mistake for a broken plan.

## Where the evidence runs thin

Honesty first, because this is where most sleep-and-training advice quietly overreaches.

The mechanism studies are small and short. Saner's protein-synthesis work ran on 24 young men over five nights. Knowles and colleagues, reviewing sleep and muscle strength for the *Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport*, found that acute sleep deprivation had **little effect on single-joint strength** and only reduced force on some multi-joint movements. So the honest read is nuanced: your max is fairly robust to a bad night, and it is repeated output and how hard the work feels that take the hit, not raw strength.

The samples are also mostly male, mostly young, mostly lab-controlled with sleep enforced far more strictly than real life. None of these studies tracked whether an under-slept lifter actually failed to add weight over a whole training block, which is the exact claim of interest here. That link, from short sleep to a stalled bar, is a reasonable inference stitched from harder-feeling sessions, lower output and slower recovery. It is not a single study that measured it end to end. Treat sleep as one strong input into your progression, not a lever that fully explains every plateau. Sometimes the bar is stuck because the programme really does need a change.

## Where Pocket Fit fits

The gap in every training log is that it records the barbell and forgets the input that decides the barbell. Pocket Fit closes that gap by keeping both in one place.

It reads your sleep from Apple Health, and after a run of short nights it flags a lighter day rather than letting you grind a plateau into a slump. Your progression still runs on the same earned logic, reps first, weight only off a confirmed two-session plateau, so nothing is inflated. Sleep does not fake progress. It just stops a bad week from being misread as a broken plan.

That sits inside the **Body budget**, a running tally of four deposits: your workout, your streak, your sleep and your nutrition. Sleep is not a separate wellness gimmick off in another app. It sits right next to the training it affects, so a stalled lift and a run of four-hour nights show up on the same screen, where the connection is obvious. The **Fuel** tool covers the other input that gates growth, your [protein](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/progressive-overload-and-protein), with no red numbers and no shame, and the programme updates week to week from what you actually log.

**[Break a real plateau with Pocket Fit](https://www.pocket-fit.app/pricing).** It only adds weight when your reps have earned it. Free on iOS and Android.

## Put it together

Progressive overload is not a promise to add weight every week. It is a queue: earn the reps, hold the plateau, then bank a small honest jump. The engine that runs it needs a signal, and the signal is you completing clean, climbing reps.

Short sleep is the variable that quietly cuts that signal off. Craven's meta-analysis puts the average performance hit at 7.56%, Saner's work shows muscle building slowing by around 18% under five short nights, and Knowles reminds us the damage lands on output and effort more than on raw max strength. A run of short nights makes your working sets fall a rep short, so the engine never gets the plateau-break it was waiting for, and you blame a plan that was never broken.

Pocket Fit's job is to keep the training and the sleep on the same page. The AI coach rebuilds a session from a sentence, the scheduler reshuffles a missed workout back into your week instead of dropping it, Fuel guards your protein, and the Body budget keeps your sleep sitting next to the lifts it decides. Georgi, who built it, went from 122 kg to competing at The Yard Games, losing 38 kg along the way, and the app exists because of the bad weeks as much as the good ones. You can read that in [our story](https://www.pocket-fit.app/our-story).

**[Start with Pocket Fit, free](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** It logs the input your plateau has been trying to tell you about. Personalised in minutes on [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).

## Sleep and progressive overload: common questions

### Why am I not progressing in the gym even though my programme looks fine?

Because progression is earned by climbing reps, and short sleep quietly stops the reps from climbing. Craven's meta-analysis found acute sleep loss cuts physical performance by an average of 7.56%, which is enough to turn your set of eight into a set of seven. If your working sets keep falling a rep short after a run of short nights, the plan is not broken. The input driving it is.

### Is a training plateau a sign my progressive overload is not working?

Not necessarily. A confirmed plateau, the same reps at the same load across two sessions, is exactly the signal a good engine waits for before adding weight. The real question is why the reps stalled. If it lines up with a week of poor sleep, the plateau is a recovery signal, not a programming failure.

### How much does sleep actually affect strength and recovery for muscle growth?

Your absolute one-rep strength is fairly robust to a bad night. What takes the hit is repeated output and how hard the work feels, plus recovery: Saner's study found muscle protein synthesis dropped by around 18% under five nights of four-hour sleep. So sleep matters most for the working sets and the rebuild between them, which is exactly where progression lives.

### Can one good night of sleep fix a stalled lift?

Rarely. The evidence points at runs of nights, not single ones. Craven's and Saner's effects came from repeated and consecutive sleep loss, so it is the fourth and fifth short night in a row that stalls your reps. The fix is a steady, repeatable bedtime across the week, not one hero lie-in.

### Should I skip the gym after a few nights of bad sleep?

No. A bad night is not a rest day. In Saner's study the group who trained through five short nights held their muscle building rate at baseline. Keep the session, go slightly lighter, hit clean reps and protect your top sets rather than skipping, so you come out of the week with your reps and your progression intact.

## References

DOI: [10.1113/JP278828](https://doi.org/10.1113/JP278828)

blog.title: Progressive Overload Runs on Protein: Why the Bar Stops Climbing When You Under-Eat
blog.description: The plateau that will not break is usually a fuelling problem, not a programming one. Under-eat, especially under-protein, and the reps stop climbing, so the bar never earns its next jump.
blog.body:
You have been running the same programme for weeks. The plan is sound, the effort is there, and yet the bar has not moved. Last month you were adding reps almost every session. Now the same weight that felt manageable grinds to a halt at rep eight, and the tenth rep you used to own has quietly disappeared. So you do the obvious thing. You question the programme. You add a set, swap an exercise, chase a new split, maybe buy a fancier plan.

Here is the assumption that traps most people. When the weight stops climbing, they treat it as a training problem and go looking for a better programme. Most of the time it is not a training problem at all. It is a fuelling problem wearing a training problem's clothes. You cannot add to the bar until your reps climb, and you cannot keep hitting those reps if you are under-fed and under-recovered. Protein for muscle growth is not a supplement-shelf nicety. It is the thing that lets progressive overload happen in the first place.

**[Train smarter with Pocket Fit](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** It reads your logged reps and adds to the bar only when you have earned it, so you can see straight away whether the stall is your programme or your plate. Free on [the App Store](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats), no card needed.

## How progression actually gets earned

Progressive overload does not mean adding weight on a schedule. It means the demand on your muscles has to keep rising, and the cleanest way to raise it is to do more work with the load already in front of you. Pocket Fit's progression engine follows that order exactly, and it is worth understanding because it explains why fuelling matters so much.

Reps come first. When you finish a set, the engine looks at your history and, if you are ready, nudges the target up by one rep at a time, never more, and never past a cap of fifteen. Only once you have held the same weight across two sessions in a row, a confirmed plateau rather than a single hard day, does the weight itself move. And it moves in small, equipment-specific steps: a barbell goes up 5 kg for men and 2.5 kg for women, while dumbbells, cables and everything else move in 2.5 kg or 1.25 kg steps by sex. If your reps drop instead of climb, the engine suggests nothing and waits.

Read that back and the dependency becomes obvious. The whole system is driven by one thing you control on the gym floor: your ability to add a rep. Add reps, and eventually the bar earns its jump. Stop adding reps, and the engine has nothing to act on. The bar sits still not because the rule failed, but because you stopped feeding it the signal. And the single most common reason the reps stop climbing has nothing to do with your programme.

## Why protein protects the reps and the muscle

Muscle is built from protein and it is broken down and rebuilt constantly. Training tips that balance towards building, but only if the raw material is there. When you chase eating for progressive overload without enough protein, you ask the body to remodel a wall while withholding the bricks.

The evidence for how much this matters is unusually clean. Robert Morton and colleagues pooled 49 randomised trials with 1,863 participants for the *British Journal of Sports Medicine* and found that adding protein to a resistance-training programme produced meaningful extra gains in strength and fat-free mass beyond training alone. The interesting number was the ceiling. Benefit kept rising with intake up to roughly **1.6 g of protein per kilogram of bodyweight per day**, and past that point the meta-regression could no longer detect an advantage. In plain terms: below that threshold you are leaving reps on the table, and above it you are not buying much more.

This is the mechanism behind the stalled bar. Under-fed muscle recovers slower and less completely between sessions. The set that should have given you a ninth rep gives you eight, the plateau never resolves upward, and the engine never gets its cue to add weight. Fix the protein and the reps often start creeping back on their own, which is exactly the signal the progression rule is waiting for. If you want the mechanics of that reps-first rule in full, we walk through it in [reps first, then weight](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/progressive-overload-reps-first-then-weight).

## How much protein to build muscle

The honest answer to how much protein to build muscle is a range, not a magic number, and it is a process target rather than a promise. Nobody can tell you that eating a precise amount will add a precise amount of muscle by a precise date. What the evidence supports is a floor to clear and a band to aim for.

- **Around 1.6 g per kg of bodyweight per day** is the intake at which Morton's meta-analysis stopped detecting extra benefit for muscle and strength. Treat it as a sensible target, not a hard cap.
- **A practical band of roughly 1.6 to 2.2 g per kg** covers most lifters and gives you room on higher-training days without obsessing over decimals.
- **When you are dieting, aim towards the upper end.** In a deficit, protein does double duty, and skimping is where lifters quietly lose the muscle they trained for.
- **Spread it across the day** rather than stacking it all at dinner. Three or four protein-forward meals is easier to hit and easier to digest than one enormous one.

None of this requires weighing every gram. It requires knowing, roughly, whether you cleared the floor today. That is a much smaller ask than most people think, and it is the one Pocket Fit's Fuel tool is built to answer.

**[Hit your protein target with Pocket Fit](https://www.pocket-fit.app/pricing).** Fuel shows whether you cleared the floor today without turning dinner into a spreadsheet. Free on iOS and Android.

## Training in a calorie deficit and strength

Now the part most articles get wrong in one direction or the other. Some tell you that you cannot build muscle while losing fat, which is too pessimistic. Others promise easy "recomp" for everyone, which is too generous. The truth sits in between, and it is genuinely encouraging if you keep protein high.

Thomas Longland and colleagues put 40 young men through a brutal four-week programme for the *American Journal of Clinical Nutrition*: a 40 percent calorie deficit, six days of hard training a week, and either 1.2 or 2.4 g of protein per kilogram. Both groups lost fat. But the higher-protein group **gained 1.2 kg of lean mass while losing 4.8 kg of fat**, and the lower-protein group merely held its lean mass while losing 3.5 kg. Same deficit, same training, radically different body composition, decided almost entirely by protein.

The message for the calorie deficit and strength question is measured. In a deficit you can often still progress, especially if you are newer or carrying some fat to lose, but progress is slower and less certain than in a surplus. Recovery is tighter, so some sessions the reps will not climb, and that is not failure. What you are really buying with high protein in a deficit is protection: you keep the muscle and the strength you already earned instead of dieting it away. For how that plays out session to session, see [calorie deficit and strength training](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/calorie-deficit-and-strength-training).

## Where the evidence runs thin

The case for protein is strong, but honesty means naming its edges. Morton's threshold of 1.6 g per kg is a population average with a wide confidence interval, and the trials leaned towards younger, mostly male, resistance-training participants. Your own number could sit higher, and older lifters in particular may need more to get the same effect.

Longland's study is striking, but it was four weeks long, in young trained men, under laboratory-tight conditions, with protein intakes that are hard to sustain in normal life. Short trials cannot tell you what a year looks like. Helms and colleagues, reviewing dietary protein for lean athletes in a deficit for the *International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism*, argued that very lean, heavily dieting athletes may need intakes towards the top of the range, scaled to leanness. That is a caveat, not a universal prescription, and most people reading this are not contest-lean.

And protein is necessary, not sufficient. Total calories, sleep, training quality and consistency all sit alongside it. Eat plenty of protein but sleep four hours and under-recover, and the reps still will not climb; our piece on [sleep and fat loss](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/sleep-and-fat-loss) covers that side. Protein removes one of the most common brakes on progress. It does not remove all of them.

## Where Pocket Fit fits

Everything above comes down to a single loop: eat enough protein, keep earning reps, let the bar climb. The hard part is not knowing the loop. It is knowing, on any given ordinary Tuesday, whether you actually cleared your protein floor and whether the stall in your log is fuelling or programming. That is the gap Pocket Fit is built to close.

Fuel is the nutrition side. Scan a plate or a barcode and it shows your protein, carbs and fat against your target, with no red numbers and no shame, just a clear read on whether you hit the floor today. On the training side, the progression engine watches your logged reps and only adds to the bar off a confirmed two-session plateau, so a stalled bar becomes a diagnostic rather than a mystery. Reps climbing but the bar not moving yet means the system is working. Reps not climbing at all points you straight back at the plate. If you want to log meals in seconds, [logging food with a photo](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/how-to-log-food-with-a-photo) shows how.

Both feed the Body budget: a running tally of four deposits, your workout, your streak, your sleep and your nutrition, so progress is one honest picture rather than four apps you have to reconcile yourself. The programme updates week to week from what you log, so as your reps and your eating change, the plan changes with them. Pocket Fit exists because the founder lived the bad weeks. Georgi went from 122 kg to competing at The Yard Games, losing 38 kg along the way, and learned first-hand that the plate decides whether the training sticks. You can read that in [our story](https://www.pocket-fit.app/our-story).

**[Start with Pocket Fit, free](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** Line up your protein and your progression in one place and let the bar climb on its own schedule. Personalised in minutes on [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).

## Protein and progressive overload: common questions

### How much protein do I need to build muscle?

Aim for roughly 1.6 to 2.2 g per kilogram of bodyweight per day. Morton's meta-analysis of 49 trials found benefit for muscle and strength rising up to about 1.6 g per kg and no clear extra gain above it, so treat that as your floor rather than a ceiling and lean towards the upper band when you are dieting.

### Can I get stronger in a calorie deficit?

Often yes, just more slowly and less reliably than in a surplus. Longland's trial had men gain 1.2 kg of lean mass in a 40 percent deficit on high protein, so progress is possible, but the main thing high protein buys in a deficit is keeping the muscle and strength you already have.

### Why has my bar stopped moving even though my programme is fine?

Because the bar only moves after your reps climb and hold for two sessions. If you are under-fed or under-protein, recovery lags, the reps stall, and the progression rule never gets the signal to add weight. The stall is often on your plate, not in your plan.

### Is more protein always better?

No. Morton's data could not detect extra muscle or strength benefit beyond about 1.6 g per kg per day for most trained people. Very lean athletes deep in a diet may need more, per Helms and colleagues, but for most lifters, piling on protein past the range mainly displaces other food rather than adding muscle.

### Does protein matter more for muscle growth or fat loss?

It matters for both, and most in a deficit. When you are eating for progressive overload in a surplus, adequate protein lets you keep earning reps. When you are cutting, higher protein is what preserves lean mass while the fat comes off, which is exactly what Longland's higher-protein group demonstrated.

## References

1. Morton RW, Murphy KT, McKellar SR, et al. (2018). A systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression of the effect of protein supplementation on resistance training-induced gains in muscle mass and strength in healthy adults. *British Journal of Sports Medicine*, 52(6), 376-384. DOI: [10.1136/bjsports-2017-097608](https://doi.org/10.1136/bjsports-2017-097608)
2. Longland TM, Oikawa SY, Mitchell CJ, Devries MC, Phillips SM (2016). Higher compared with lower dietary protein during an energy deficit combined with intense exercise promotes greater lean mass gain and fat mass loss: a randomized trial. *American Journal of Clinical Nutrition*, 103(3), 738-746. DOI: [10.3945/ajcn.115.119339](https://doi.org/10.3945/ajcn.115.119339)
3. Helms ER, Zinn C, Rowlands DS, Brown SR (2014). A systematic review of dietary protein during caloric restriction in resistance trained lean athletes: a case for higher intakes. *International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism*, 24(2), 127-138. DOI: [10.1123/ijsnem.2013-0054](https://doi.org/10.1123/ijsnem.2013-0054)

Always consult a qualified healthcare professional before starting or changing a training or nutrition programme, and if you have persistent problems with sleep, pain or fatigue.*

blog.title: Reps First, Then Weight: The Progressive Overload Rule That Actually Adds to the Bar
blog.description: Progressive overload is not "add weight every week". Add reps first, and the bar only moves after you have held the same load for two sessions in a row.
blog.body:
You add 2.5 kg to the bar every Monday because that is what progressive overload means, right? Add weight, get stronger, repeat. For the first month it works. Then a session gets ugly, the reps fall apart, your form goes with them, and the next week you are staring at a load you cannot really lift. So you back off, feel like you failed, and quietly wonder whether the whole thing works at all.

Here is the part that gets missed. Progressive overload is real and it is the single most important idea in training. But "add weight every week" is not what it says. That is a caricature of it, and it is the reason so many people stall or get hurt inside two months. The actual rule is slower, more boring, and far more reliable. You earn the weight jump, you do not schedule it.

**[Train smarter with Pocket Fit](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** It applies the reps-first progression rule to your logged sets automatically, so you never have to guess when to add to the bar. Free on [the App Store](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Google Play](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats), no card needed.

## What progressive overload actually is

Progressive overload just means the demand on your muscles has to keep rising over time, or they have no reason to change. That is it. The demand can rise in several ways: more weight, more reps, more sets, better technique, less rest. Weight on the bar is only one of them, and it is the one you should reach for last, not first.

The evidence for the underlying principle is strong. Schoenfeld, Ogborn and Krieger pooled 15 studies for the *Journal of Sports Sciences* and found a clear graded relationship between how much work you do and how much muscle you build. Each additional weekly set was linked to roughly a **0.37% larger gain** in muscle size. More total work, more growth, in a fairly straight line across the range they studied. The lesson is not "lift heavier", it is "do more good work over time". Reps are work. Sets are work. Weight is work. They all count.

So the question is never really "when do I add weight". It is "how do I keep making the sessions a little harder without breaking myself". Adding weight is the crudest, riskiest lever. Adding reps is the gentle one, and it is where you should start.

## How to progressive overload: reps first, then weight

The method that does this cleanly is called double progression, and it is exactly how Pocket Fit works. You pick a weight and a rep target. You add reps first, one at a time, session by session, until you hit the top of the range. Only then does the weight go up, and when it does, your reps reset to the bottom of the range so you can climb again.

In Pocket Fit the rep cap is **15**. Say you press a dumbbell for 8 reps. Next time the app asks for 9, then 10, and so on, adding a single rep at a time as you clear each one. It never jumps you from 8 to 12. One rep is a small, honest step you can almost always make, which is the whole point - you want a target you can beat, not one you have to gamble on. When you finally reach 15 clean reps, the weight goes up and you drop back down the rep ladder.

This matters because reps are where most of the growth lives anyway. Schoenfeld, Grgic, Ogborn and Krieger ran a separate meta-analysis in the *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research* comparing lighter loads for higher reps against heavier loads for lower reps. When sets were taken close to failure, muscle growth was **near enough identical** whether the load was light or heavy. Heavier loads had an edge for maximal strength, but for building muscle, chasing reps at a given weight does the job. You are not leaving results on the table by grinding out reps before you touch the plates. You are doing the thing that works.

## Why the bar only moves off a confirmed plateau

Here is the part that separates a sane rule from wishful thinking. Pocket Fit will not add weight the moment you hit your rep cap once. It waits until you have held the same load for two sessions in a row - a confirmed plateau - before it adds anything. One good session can be a fluke: good sleep, good coffee, a friend spotting you. Two in a row at the same weight is a pattern, and a pattern is what you build on.

Just as important, it never stacks two jumps back to back. If you progressed last session, the app suggests nothing new this time. It lets the new load settle before asking for more. This is the guardrail the "add weight every Monday" crowd never had. They were stacking jump on jump on jump, and the reason it fell apart in month two was not weakness, it was arithmetic. Nobody adds 2.5 kg a week to a lift for a year.

Why be this careful about proximity to real effort? Because getting close to your limit is what drives the adaptation, and it is also where the risk lives. Refalo and colleagues examined this in *Sports Medicine*, pooling 15 studies on how close to failure you train. Training right to failure gave only a **trivial** extra edge for muscle growth over stopping a rep or two short. In plain terms: you do not need to grind every set into the ground. Leaving a rep in the tank most of the time, and letting the weight rise only when you have genuinely earned it, gives you almost all of the benefit with far less of the damage.

**[See your progress build with Pocket Fit](https://www.pocket-fit.app/pricing).** The Progress view shows every lift climbing rep by rep, so a plateau is obvious the moment it happens. Free on iOS and Android.

## The steps are small on purpose

When the weight does go up, the jump is deliberately tiny, and it depends on the equipment. In Pocket Fit a barbell moves by **5 kg for men and 2.5 kg for women**. Everything else - dumbbells, cables, machines - moves by **2.5 kg for men and 1.25 kg for women**. That is not an accident or a limitation. Small is the point.

Think about what a jump actually asks of you in percentage terms. Adding 2.5 kg to a 40 kg dumbbell press is a 6% rise. Adding 5 kg to a 100 kg squat is 5%. Those are steps you can absorb and repeat. Now picture the beginner who reads "add weight" and slaps a 10 kg plate on each side - a 20 kg jump. The reps collapse, the form breaks, and the "progression" lasts exactly one session before it reverses. A dumbbell gets a smaller absolute step than a barbell because you cannot spread the load across both hands the way a barbell lets you, so the same absolute jump hits harder. The rule respects that.

And it changes one thing at a time. Reps or weight, never both at once. If both moved together you would never know which change your body was responding to, or which one broke the set. One variable, one clean signal, every session.

## The "too easy" override, and when the rule should bend

A fixed rule needs an escape hatch, or it becomes a cage. Pocket Fit has one. If a set was genuinely trivial, you flag it as too easy, and the app bumps the weight early - it does not make you sit through two plateau sessions to prove what you already know. You earned the jump by clearing the work with obvious room to spare, so it gives it to you. That is the one case where the weight moves without a confirmed plateau.

The rule should also bend the other way. It only ever suggests going up or holding; it does not chase you down when you have a bad week. If your reps drop because you slept badly or came in stressed, the honest move is to hold the load, not add to it, and not to read one rough session as a plateau. A run of short nights genuinely changes what your body can do that day, which is worth understanding on its own - we wrote about that in [why a bad night is not a rest day](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/sleep-and-muscle-growth-bad-night-not-a-rest-day). And every few months, deliberately backing off - a deload, lighter loads for a week - is not lost progress, it is what lets the next climb happen. Progressive overload is a long game with pauses built in, not a line that only ever goes up.

## Where Pocket Fit fits: the rule runs itself

The trouble with double progression is not the idea, it is the bookkeeping. You have to remember last session's weight and reps, the one before that, which lifts are at their cap, which are mid-plateau, and what the right step is for a dumbbell versus a barbell versus a woman versus a man. People try to hold that in their head or in a notebook, and they drift. Pocket Fit does not drift, because it is deterministic - the same logged sets always produce the same next step.

You do not decide when to add weight. You log your sets, and the engine reads your last two sessions and applies the rule: add a rep, or hold, or - off a confirmed plateau - add the specific step for that piece of kit. It is the same logic every time, no mood, no guessing. Your programme updates week to week from what you actually logged, and the AI coach can rebuild a session from a sentence if you need to swap an exercise or train around a niggle. Each earned rep and each earned plate feeds your Body budget, the running tally of your workout, streak, sleep and nutrition, so the slow honest progress shows up as something you can see.

This is exactly why the app exists. Georgi built Pocket Fit after going from 122 kg to competing at The Yard Games, losing 38 kg along the way - and the thing that got him there was not heroic weekly jumps, it was small earned steps that did not reverse. You can read that story on [our story page](https://www.pocket-fit.app/our-story). If you are eating in a deficit while you do it, the same patience applies to the food side too, which we covered in [calorie deficit and strength training](https://www.pocket-fit.app/blog/calorie-deficit-and-strength-training).

## Where this rule runs thin

Be honest about the limits. Double progression with a two-session plateau check is a sane default, not a law of physics. It will not be optimal for everyone in every phase.

Progress is not linear forever. In your first months on a lift you might climb almost every session. A year in, adding a single rep can take weeks, and the neat ladder gets lumpy. The rule still points the right way, but the pace slows and no engine can change that. Individual variation is real too - people recover at different rates, respond to different rep ranges, and some lifts move faster than others on the same body. A fixed 15-rep cap and a fixed step are reasonable middle-ground choices, not personalised prescriptions. And the research behind all this, strong as it is, measures group averages over weeks and months. It tells you the direction that works for most people. It does not promise your exact number next Tuesday.

The rule earns its place not by being perfect but by being reliable and safe across the huge middle of cases. It stops you stacking jumps, it makes the steps small, and it only adds weight you have shown you can handle. For most people, most of the time, that beats a clever plan you cannot stick to.

**[Start with Pocket Fit, free](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785).** It runs the reps-first progression rule off your logged sets, so the bar only ever moves when you have earned it. Personalised in minutes on [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/gb/app/pocket-squats/id6748518785) and [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.pocketsquats).

## Progressive overload: common questions

### How do you do progressive overload as a beginner?

Do not add weight every week. Pick a weight and a rep target, then add one rep at a time each session until you reach the top of the range - in Pocket Fit that cap is 15. Only then does the weight go up, and only after you have held the same load steady. This double progression is gentler and far more reliable than chasing heavier plates from day one.

### When should I increase the weight I lift?

When you have hit the top of your rep range and held the same load for two sessions in a row - a confirmed plateau. One good session can be luck. Two in a row is a pattern worth building on. The one exception is when a set is genuinely too easy, in which case bumping earlier is fine.

### What is double progression?

Double progression means you progress two variables in order, not at once. First you add reps at a fixed weight, one rep per session up to a cap. Then, once you top out and plateau, you add a small amount of weight and drop back down the rep range to climb again. Reps first, then weight.

### How much weight should I add to the bar?

A small, equipment-specific step. Pocket Fit adds 5 kg to a barbell for men and 2.5 kg for women, and 2.5 kg for men or 1.25 kg for women on dumbbells, cables and machines. Small steps are the point - a 2.5 kg jump on a dumbbell press is only about 6%, which you can absorb and repeat, whereas a big jump collapses your reps and reverses within a session.

### Does adding reps build as much muscle as adding weight?

For muscle size, largely yes. A meta-analysis in the *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research* found that lighter loads for higher reps and heavier loads for lower reps produced near-identical growth when sets were taken close to failure. Heavier loads had an edge only for maximal strength. So grinding out reps at a given weight before you add plates is not a compromise, it is doing the work that matters.

## References

1. Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW (2017). Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass: a systematic review and meta-analysis. *Journal of Sports Sciences*, 35(11), 1073-1082. DOI: [10.1080/02640414.2016.1210197](https://doi.org/10.1080/02640414.2016.1210197)
2. Schoenfeld BJ, Grgic J, Ogborn D, Krieger JW (2017). Strength and hypertrophy adaptations between low- vs. high-load resistance training: a systematic review and meta-analysis. *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research*, 31(12), 3508-3523. DOI: [10.1519/JSC.0000000000002200](https://doi.org/10.1519/JSC.0000000000002200)
3. Refalo MC, Helms ER, Trexler ET, Hamilton DL, Fyfe JJ (2023). Influence of resistance training proximity-to-failure on skeletal muscle hypertrophy: a systematic review with meta-analysis. *Sports Medicine*, 53(3), 649-665. DOI: [10.1007/s40279-022-01784-y](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40279-022-01784-y)
